coredata.dc:title: Ultrahighly-efficient and stable luminescent solar concentrators enabled by FRET-based carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiurong
Zheng, Shuyang
Zhao, Xiujian
Vomiero, Alberto
Gong, Xiao
coredata.dc:description:
Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) as an optical device to concentrate light from large areas illuminated by sunlight into small-area photovoltaic cells, can greatly reduce the cost of solar energy generation, thus showing promise in building-integrated photovoltaics. Here, we report the synthesis of a novel type of composite nanoparticle via the simple sol-gel method. Starting from hydroxyl-rich carbon nanodots (CDs), Rhodamine B (RhB) molecules were embedded into a silica matrix shell, forming a composite core-shell system, where the CDs represent the core and RhB embedded in SiO2 are the shell of the final CDs/RhB@SiO2 structure. These nanoparticles were successfully employed in high-performance LSC fabrication. The Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) between the CDs and the dye was successfully exploited to improve the light absorption efficiency and power conversion efficiency (η
PCE ) of LSC devices. The solid-state photoluminescence quantum yield of the as-synthesized composite nanoparticles can reach ∼ 57 % due to the effective suppression of the aggregation-induced quenching. These fluorescent composite nanoparticles were used for fabricating LSC devices with an ultrahigh η
PCE of ∼ 3.8 % for 5 × 5 cm2 devices, thanks to the high loading of luminophores and effective FRET.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Core-shell nanoparticles
Carbon dots (CDs) / Rhodamine B (RhB)-embedded silica matrix (CDs/RhB@SiO2)
FRET
High efficiency luminescent solar concentrators (∼3.8 %)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure of the CDs/RhB@SiO2
Energy transfer process between the CDs and RhB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of procedures for the preparation of CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite nanoparticles.
(a) Fluorescence mechanism of the CDs/RhB@SiO2 nanocomposite. TEM image and HRTEM image of CDs (b, c) and CDs/RhB@SiO2 (e, f). Size distribution of CDs (d) and CDs/RhB@SiO2 (g).

(a) XRD pattern of CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite nanoparticles. (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2. (c) Full XPS spectra of CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2.

(a) Photographs of the CDs and CDs/RhB@SiO2 powders under daylight (upper) and 365 nm UV light (lower). (b) UV–vis absorption spectra, PL excitation, and emission spectra of the CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite nanoparticles. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs/RhB@SiO2-1, CDs/RhB@SiO2-2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2-3. (d) PL emission spectra of the CDs/RhB@SiO2-1, CDs/RhB@SiO2-2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2-3. (e) FRET efficiency of the CDs and RhB mixtures in SiO2 matrix.

(a) Photographs of the film LSCs fabricated by CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2 under a 365 nm UV lamp, respectively. The dimensions of the LSCs were 5 × 5 cm2. Transmittance spectra (b), PL excitation (c), and emission spectra (d) of the film LSCs with various concentrations of CDs/RhB@SiO2. (e) PL excitation and emission spectra of the film LSCs based on CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2. (f) Relationship of the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J-V) and η
PCE of the film LSCs based on CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2. (g) Relationship of the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J-V) and η
PCE of the film LSCs with various concentrations of CDs/RhB@SiO2.

(a) η
PCE of the film LSCs based on CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2. (b) η
PCE of the film LSCs with various concentrations of CDs/RhB@SiO2 within 28 days in ambient conditions.

head.ce:title: Ultrahighly-efficient and stable luminescent solar concentrators enabled by FRET-based carbon nanodots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Materials Science, Department of Engineering Sciences and Mathematics, Luleå University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) as an optical device to concentrate light from large areas illuminated by sunlight into small-area photovoltaic cells, can greatly reduce the cost of solar energy generation, thus showing promise in building-integrated photovoltaics. Here, we report the synthesis of a novel type of composite nanoparticle via the simple sol-gel method. Starting from hydroxyl-rich carbon nanodots (CDs), Rhodamine B (RhB) molecules were embedded into a silica matrix shell, forming a composite core-shell system, where the CDs represent the core and RhB embedded in SiO2 are the shell of the final CDs/RhB@SiO2 structure. These nanoparticles were successfully employed in high-performance LSC fabrication. The Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) between the CDs and the dye was successfully exploited to improve the light absorption efficiency and power conversion efficiency (η
PCE ) of LSC devices. The solid-state photoluminescence quantum yield of the as-synthesized composite nanoparticles can reach ∼ 57 % due to the effective suppression of the aggregation-induced quenching. These fluorescent composite nanoparticles were used for fabricating LSC devices with an ultrahigh η
PCE of ∼ 3.8 % for 5 × 5 cm2 devices, thanks to the high loading of luminophores and effective FRET.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Carbon dots (CDs) / Rhodamine B (RhB)-embedded silica matrix (CDs/RhB@SiO2)

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the constant loss and even exhaustion of non-renewable energy, the need for clean and renewable energy sources is growing due to global warming caused by excessive carbon emissions. Currently, there is a great deal of interest in green energy production technologies such as wind, wave, and solar energy. Among these, solar energy is considered to be the most promising technology for creating clean, reliable, and scalable power for the future. Additionally, it is easily integrated with other renewable energy technologies such as wind or thermal solar energy [1–3]. Therefore, effectively collecting and converting sunlight into usable energy has great prospects for solving energy and environmental problems. The most common way to harness solar energy is through photovoltaic technology, which converts sunlight into electrical energy using solar cells. However, obtaining high power conversion efficiency (PCE) at a low cost is still a major challenge for solar cells.

The emergence of luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) provides a great alternative to solve this problem in the frame of the so-called building-integrated photovoltaic (BIPV) technologies [4]. The LSCs are optical devices that can provide an effective way to collect sunlight from a large area and concentrate it on the side edges of a small area. Typically, an LSC consists of a slab with transparent materials which includes a luminophore and an optical waveguide matrix. The process of collecting solar energy for conversion into electricity involves the use of luminophores which absorb sunlight and then re-emit light at longer wavelengths. To ensure efficient collection by photovoltaic (PV) cells, most of the re-emitted light is concentrated along an optical waveguide by total internal reflection. This concentrated light is then redirected into PV devices attached to the edge of the waveguide, where it is converted into usable electricity [5]. Generally, the Stokes shift of the luminophores should be large enough so that the overlap between the emission and absorption spectra is as small as possible to reduce reabsorption losses during the propagation of photons along the waveguiding pathway in LSCs, thus ensuring the best LSC performance. Reabsorption is particularly harmful to the performance of LSCs since it will lead to non-radiative energy losses [6]. For this reason, strong research efforts have been made by selecting appropriate luminophores to optimize the Stoke shift.

To date, various luminophores such as organic dyes/polymers, semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs), perovskite nanocrystals, and carbon nanodots (CDs) have been hired to manufacture LSC devices that are highly efficient [7]. However, the organic dyes/polymers and perovskite nanocrystals generally suffer from inefficient trapping, high reabsorption due to the small Stokes shift, and poor photo-stability under light illumination [8–10]. Although SQDs have become promising luminescent species as luminophores in LSCs due to their unique optical properties, most SQDs contain toxic heavy metal elements (e.g., Pb and Cd), which are significantly harmful to the natural environment and human health [11–13]. Thus, it is urgent to pursue a green, non-toxic phosphor material with excellent photoluminescence properties (such as adjustable excitation and emission, large Stokes shift, high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) and excellent long-term photochemical stability) for LSC fabrication [8,9,14,15]. The emergence of CDs makes up for this defect, and CDs are regarded as promising candidates as LSC emitters because of their unique and outstanding properties, such as tunable emission spectrum, broad absorption, high PLQYs, excellent photostability, environmentally-friendliness, simple preparation and low cost [16–21]. In 2017, Gong group for the first time integrated nitrogen-doped CDs in efficient LSCs [22]. Subsequently, intense research on CDs as luminophores for LSCs developed very fast [23–31]. Although the use of CDs as luminophores has certain advantages, there are also some limitations (e.g., long wavelength emissive CDs with small Stokes shift and low PLQY) that will lead to energy loss and low efficiency. In recent years, numerous reports have demonstrated that the losses of LSCs can be minimized by utilizing Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) in their design, which is able to lead a higher device efficiency [32–39]. FRET is a process where excess energy from an excited species (donor) is transferred to an acceptor species (acceptor) through space, mediated by dipole-dipole coupling [38]. At present, FRET between singlet states of chromophores in multi-fluorophore assemblies has been extensively studied to obtain fluorescent materials with tunable wavelengths [40–43]. According to the FRET theory, the probability of energy transfer is proportional to the spectral overlap integral of the donor-acceptor system [44,45]. Therefore, the absorption range of luminescent materials can be broadened by pre-designing suitable energy donors and acceptors to capture sunlight as much as possible. More importantly, the silica matrix is a favorable alternative in the process of energy transfer as it is a good intermediate transfer medium [45–48]. In 2021, Corsini et al. first used silica-based dye core-shell nanoparticles as luminophores to manufacture efficient and stable LSCs (G = 2.08) with a maximum PCE of 1.073 % but they did not achieve FRET [49]. Tummelshammer et al. were the first to demonstrate experimentally that the attachment of inorganic fluorophores to organic fluorophores improves the performance of LSCs with a maximum PCE of 2.87 % via a FRET process in 2017 [33]. In 2019, Lyu et al. [50] prepared LSCs by covalently linking AIE molecules to dye molecules (G = 3.75), achieving an external optical efficiency of 5.5 %. In 2021, Delgado-Sanchez et al. [3] designed LSCs based on FRET between dyes with a PCE of 1.82 %. However, the use of toxic heavy metals in this application can be harmful to both human health and the environment.

In this work, we demonstrate a novel type of eco-friendly composite nanoparticles by confining the strong green emissive CDs and Rhodamine B (RhB) in a silica encapsulation layer (CDs/RhB@SiO2), which aims to achieve enhanced performance of LSC devices. In this system, green emissive CDs with a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface act as energy donors, which can form covalent bonds with silica. The organic dye RhB with a positively charged surface acts as an energy acceptor, which is effectively confined in the silica nanoparticles due to electrostatic interaction. Herein, RhB acts as an energy acceptor that transforms high-energy green photons emitted by CDs into low-energy red photons through the FRET process. The extremely high FRET efficiency (∼ 86 %) between CDs and RhB due to their high spectral overlap plays a crucial role in enhancing the performance of LSCs. The experimental results show that the FRET process significantly improves the light absorption efficiency while decreasing the reabsorption loss. Furthermore, the optical efficiency can be enhanced by tuning the concentration of donor and acceptor in the LSCs. This work is expected to provide a new proof of concept for the realization of high-performance LSC devices using the FRET process, which opens up a new route for the future development of LSC devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of the CDs/RhB@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain highly luminescent FRET-based composite nanoparticle materials, it is crucial to select a suitable energy donor and receptor. In addition, to avoid the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs in the composite process, the employed CDs must have suitable surface functional groups, suitable luminescence wavelengths, and large Stokes shifts. For this reason, the selection of suitable CDs is of paramount importance to realize the whole FRET process. Here, the required CDs were first synthesized according to our previous report (see Experimental Section for details) [28]. CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal approach, and the prepared CDs have been shown to have abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface, which form stable colloidal dispersions in ethanol. Subsequently, CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite nanoparticles were synthesized based on the Stöber method, while cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was added to form micelles to improve the dispersion of the dye molecules, and then we developed luminescent composites through chemical reaction between tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) and the CDs. Donors and acceptors for energy transfer are constructed by confining CDs with green fluorescence emission and RhB with orange-red fluorescence emission in a silica shell. The related synthetic scheme is shown in Fig. 1.

Energy transfer usually occurs between a donor and an acceptor. Fig. 2a illustrates the process of FRET between donor CDs and acceptor RhB. Due to the dipole-dipole interaction, the excited state of CDs will be deactivated, which leads to the generation of many excited states of RhB. Thus, energy will be released through long wavelength emission. Therefore, orange-red fluorescence can be achieved through FRET. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were utilized to determine the morphological and structural properties of CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite nanoparticles. Fig. 2b shows that the synthesized CDs were monodispersed spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 5.3 nm (Fig. 2d). As shown in Fig. 2c, the HRTEM image of CDs exhibited well-resolved fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is attributed to the (001) facet of graphite[51]. To maintain the high quantum yield (QY) of CDs in the solid state, CDs and RhB were confined in SiO2 nanoparticles by hydrolyzing TEOS in aqueous CTAB micelle solutions. As shown in Figs. 2e and 2g, the TEM image reveals the monodispersed CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite nanoparticles with an average particle size of 21.5 nm and a clear spherical morphology. The HRTEM image shows the clear presence of CDs crystals in amorphous silica composite nanoparticles, indicating that CDs were successfully confined in the silica shell (Fig. 2f).

Furthermore, the synthesized nanocomposites were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to better analyze the components and structure of the obtained materials. The XRD pattern of CDs shows a dominant broad peak near 21.5°, which is attributed to reflections from the (002) surface of graphitic carbon (Figure S1) [52]. As shown in Fig. 3a, compared to the XRD of pure CDs, the XRD of CDs@SiO2 (green line) was found to almost overlap with it. The slight shift to larger angles in the CDs@SiO2 sample, compared to CDs can be easily explained with a compressive stress of SiO2 on the graphitic carbon structure [45]. When RhB was encapsulated inside SiO2, the XRD of RhBs@SiO2 (red line) presented two distinct characteristic peaks located at approximately 21° and 24.3°, respectively. The former may be derived from the compressive stress changes produced by SiO2-encapsulated CDs which may cause a slight shift of the peak position, while the latter may originate from the effect of RhB, i.e., RhB may have produced a self-crystallization or induced crystallization process. Likewise, the XRD pattern of the sample CDs/RhB@SiO2 (yellow line) presents almost the same peak shape as the RhBs@SiO2 sample. These results suggest that CDs and RhB may be encapsulated by a SiO2 shell [53]. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was also employed to clearly characterize the morphological structure of the CDs/RhB@SiO2 samples, indicating that the synthesized nanocomposites were quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a narrow size distribution (Figure S2). The FT-IR spectra of CDs reveal a broad absorption band at approximately 3416 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of -OH/-NH, while the vibrational peaks at 1515–1800 cm−1 are due to the -CH2 and N-H bonds on the surface of the CDs (Figure S3) [54]. The functional groups on CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2 were also characterized by FT-IR as shown in Fig. 3b. The FT-IR spectra of CDs@SiO2 had a broad absorption band at 3416 cm−1 ascribed to the stretching vibration of -OH/-NH, while the vibrational peaks at 1515–1800 cm−1 were due to the -CH2 and N-H bonds on the surface of the CDs [55]. The FT-IR spectra of CDs/RhB@SiO2 showed the presence of Si-OH (3416 cm−1) and Si-O-Si (1070 cm−1) stretching vibrations as well as the characteristic C-Si bonds at 790 cm−1, respectively, indicating that CDs are encapsulated by SiO2 shell and covalent bonds are formed between CDs and external SiO2 [56–58]. Additionally, The full XPS spectrum of the CDs/RhB@SiO2 shows the presence of C, N, O, Cl, and Si elements (Fig. 3c). XPS results indicate successful encapsulation of CDs via the presence of C, N, and O elements. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Si 2 s for the CDs/RhB@SiO2 sample are depicted in Figure S4. C 1 s spectrum can be fitted into two small peaks, 286.3 eV (C-Si) and 284.8 eV (C-C), respectively. The N 1 s spectrum can be discretized into two small peaks corresponding to C-N (398.9 eV) and N-H (401.8 eV). Both Si-O (532.6 eV) and O-H (531.8 eV) can be observed in the O 1 s spectrum. The Si 2 s spectrum has two peaks at 102.8 and 103.6 eV, which may originate from Si-O and C-Si bonds. The above results suggest that the CDs and RhB were confined in a silica shell [59,60]. The element composition of the CDs/RhB@SiO2 was also analyzed by EDS, as indicated in Figure S5. The C, N, O, Si, and Cl elements can be obtained from the sample, which is consistent with the XPS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy transfer process between the CDs and RhB
ce:section.ce:para: According to FRET theory, the energy transfer process originates from dipole-dipole interactions, so the following conditions need to be met for energy transfer to occur between CDs and RhB [61]. The process of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) involves three key requirements. Firstly, there should be enough spectral overlap between the emission of the donor and the absorption of the acceptor. Secondly, the distance between the donor and acceptor molecules must be less than 10 nm. Finally, the dipoles of the donor and acceptor should be aligned favorably towards each other for effective energy transfer to occur. Simultaneous encapsulation of CDs and RhB into amorphous silica during TEOS hydrolysis enables effective FRET due to proximity. To investigate the energy transfer process between CDs and RhB, the photophysical properties of CDs, CDs@SiO2, and RhB@SiO2 were measured (Figure S6). The UV–vis absorption spectra of the donors (CDs) show two prominent absorption peaks at approximately 300 nm and 350 nm. The peak at 300 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition, while the peak at 350 nm may be due to the n-π* absorption (as shown in Figure S6a) [62]. The maximum PL excitation and emission peaks of CDs were located at 375 nm and 510 nm, respectively. Upon increasing the excitation wavelength, the emission peak displayed a clear redshift (see Figure S6d). Typically, CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emissions due to multiple energy transitions. After the CDs were encapsulated by the SiO2 shell, the absorption peak of CDs@SiO2 was shifted from the original CDs near 350 nm to 380 nm (Figure S6b). Noticeably, the gap between absorption and emission of CDs@SiO2 was smaller than that of CDs, which indicates that SiO2 had an effect on CDs. Similarly, CDs@SiO2 also had typical excitation-dependent emission (Figure S6e). Besides, as shown in Figure S6c, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of RhB@SiO2 showed a strong absorption peak near 500 nm, which is distinctly different from the absorption recorded in pure CDs and in CDs@SiO2 complexes, suggesting that this absorption peak may be derived from RhB based on a comparison with the absorption spectrum of pure RhB (Figure S7). In addition, the excitation and emission spectra of RhB@SiO2 were also significantly different, with the optimized excitation being approximately 550 nm and the most intense emission being around 590 nm. It is noteworthy that the excitation-emission correlation of RhB encapsulated by SiO2 is clearly different from the CDs and CDs@SiO2 samples, presenting an excitation-independent emission (Figure S6f).

According to the previous analysis of the optical properties between CDs, CDs@SiO2, and RhB@SiO2, it can be found that SiO2 can successfully achieve the encapsulation of the composite of CDs and RhB. Therefore, the products of the final composite system of CDs/RhB@SiO2 were also utilized for the optical property analysis. Fig. 4a displays the photographs of the CDs and CDs/RhB@SiO2 powders under sunlight and UV light, respectively. The optical behavior of CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite nanoparticles is illustrated in Fig. 4b. Interestingly, the UV–vis absorption spectra reveal that the absorption of CDs/RhB@SiO2 was presented as a superposition of CDs and RhB after encapsulation with a SiO2 shell. Thus, distinct absorption characteristic peaks appeared near 300 nm, 350 nm, and 550 nm, which can be attributed to the successful composite cladding between CDs and RhB on SiO2. The strongest emission peak appeared at about 590 nm and was also accompanied by a shoulder peak at about 480 nm. The former can be attributed to the emission of RhB, while the latter may be derived from the emission of CDs. According to the energy transfer process, the energy delivered by the donor needs to be received by the acceptor. This phenomenon is further proving the successful combination of CDs and RhB within the SiO2 shell. The fraction of energy received by the acceptor concerning the energy emitted by the donor determines the efficiency of the overall energy transfer. Therefore, three different concentrations of acceptor (RhB, samples CDs/RhB@SiO2-1, CDs/RhB@SiO2-2, CDs/RhB@SiO2-3, see Experimental section) have also been applied for optical studies to determine the occurrence of energy transfer between CDs and RhB. As shown in Fig. 4c, the absorption spectra of CDs/RhB@SiO2 extend from 300 nm to almost 650 nm, making it ideal for capturing solar radiation more efficiently than bare CDs or RhB. With the increase of RhB concentration, a significant enhancement of the absorption peak at about 550 nm was observed. Interestingly, the corresponding emission peaks showed an inverse regularity. As the RhB concentration increased, the emission peak at RhB also increased, as shown in Fig. 4d. Conversely, the emission peaks at CDs decreased accordingly by increasing the concentration of RhB from 0 to 50 μg/mL. Meanwhile, a distinct red-shift process was also produced for the emission peak related to RhB with the increase of RhB concentration, which shifted from about 570 nm to approximately 590 nm. The fluorescence lifetime also reflects the relationship between the concentration of the materials. Figure S9 shows the lifetime decay curves of the CDs/RhB@SiO2-x sample series (x=1, 2, 3) upon excitation at 365 nm. The fluorescence lifetime of the donor CDs monitored at 500 nm decreased from 2.5 ns to 0.7 ns, while the fluorescence lifetime of the acceptor at 590 nm increased from 2.9 ns to 4.7 ns. Furthermore, the femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy (fs-TAS) was used to further verify the relationship between the donor CDs and the acceptor RhB. 3D TA spectra of CDs/RhB@SiO2 with probe wavelengths of 350–700 nm under the excitation of 365 nm were collected and detected over a decay time of 0.3 ps - 7.8 ns, as presented in Figure S10a. Two obvious negative signals (blue regions) were observed between 400 nm and 500 nm and 550–650 nm, respectively. In addition, a positive signal (yellow region) was presented between 650 nm and 700 nm. The TA spectra with different decay times were demonstrated in Figure S10b. According to the TA spectra at different decay times, the TA peaks located at ≈420 nm, 605 nm and 660 nm can be clearly observed. Combining the UV–vis absorption and steady-state PL spectra of CDs/RhB@SiO2, the negative absorption signal is attributed to the stimulated emission (SE) which is consistent with their PL spectra. The positive absorption signal is ascribed to excited state absorption (ESA). Figure S10c shows the decay kinetics with delay time for the peak centered at 430 nm and 607 nm. These results further demonstrate that FRET occurs between the singlet state of the donor CDs and the acceptor RhB. Besides, the intensity ratio of the PL emission peak of the acceptor (RhB) and donor (CDs) increased as the RhB concentration increased. This shows that an efficient FRET process was generated between the CDs and RhB[45]. Their excitation correlation is also presented in Figure S8 . The FRET efficiency between the CDs and RhB can be calculated according to the following equation[63,64]:
(1)
E = 1 −
I
/
I
0
where I and
I
0
represent the PL intensity of the donor CDs with and without acceptor RhB, respectively. When the concentration of RhB reached 50 μg/mL, the color of the powders changed from green to orange (Fig. 4a). According to Eq. (1) and combined with Fig. 4e, the highest FRET efficiency can reach ∼ 86 %. Significantly, the optimal solid-state QY of CDs/RhB@SiO2 can reach ∼ 57 % (Figure S11). This property demonstrates the potential use of the CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite in photovoltaic applications.

Photographs of the thin-film LSCs fabricated by CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2 under a 365 nm UV lamp, respectively, are shown in Fig. 5a. PL spectra and transmittance spectra of LSC films with different CDs/RhB@SiO2 concentrations were studied to visualize luminescence performances and optimal states. As shown in Fig. 5b, the concentration has a tremendous effect on the transparency of the film, which decreases with the increasing CD concentration in the full spectral range. Remarkably, there is no change in the shape of the excitation spectrum of the film as the concentration increases, except for the change in intensity (Fig. 5c), which increases with the increasing concentration According to Fig. 5d, the PL intensity of LSCs showed a significant and continuous increase at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm when the concentration of CDs/RhB@SiO2 varied from 0.5 wt% to 9 wt%, with an increment rate of up to ∼ 93 %. This indicates that the concentration has a dramatic effect on the luminescence efficiency. For comparison and to better investigate the performance of LSC devices based on donor and acceptor FRET, thin film LSCs based on CDs@SiO2 (donor), RhB@SiO2 (acceptor), and CDs/RhB@SiO2 (donor/acceptor) with the same concentration were prepared. As shown in Fig. 5e, the optical properties of the donor and acceptor after integration into LSC devices were almost the same as their solid powder counterparts, indicating that this composite has favorable polymer matrix compatibility and excellent photostability. Ultimately, LSC devices at different concentrations were also fabricated by utilizing properties such as wide absorption range, high solid-state QY, and large Stokes shift from energy resonance transfer. The LSC PCE as one key parameter was calculated as [22,24–26,30]:
(2)
η
PCE
=
J
sc
×
V
oc
× FF
P
in
where J sc refers to the short circuit current density, V
oc is the open circuit voltage, FF is the fill factor of the solar cell coupled with the LSC, and P in refers to the irradiation intensity (100 mW cm−2, AM 1.5 G solar simulator) impinging at normal incidence on the surface of the LSC. The intensity of the photocurrent density of LSCs increased with increasing concentration, leading to a significant increase in η
PCE from 1.3 % to 3.8 % at a light intensity of 60 mW/cm2. To visualize the enhancement of the final fluorescence emission by the addition of CDs, we report in Figure S12 the PL emission from CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2 based on the PL of the LSCs devices under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm, and it is clearly observed that the intensity of PL was enhanced at the red emission with the addition of CDs. The strong difference in PL intensity is reflected in the huge gap in the efficiency performance of donor, acceptor, and donor/acceptor in LSCs devices. The J-V curves of the CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2 composites are shown in Fig. 5f, exhibiting η
PCE as high as 2.1 %, 2.6 %, and 3.8 %, respectively for a 5 × 5 × 0.2 cm3 LSC device, this value is superior to the performance with CDs-based LSCs devices (Table S1). For reference, the J-V curve of the pure Si cell is reported in Figure S13. The η
PCE value of the LSC based on donor/acceptor was significantly increased compared to that of single donor or acceptor. Fig. 5g presents the photovoltaic performance of thin-film structured LSC with different concentrations of CD/RhB@SiO2 at a light intensity of 60 mW/cm2. It can be clearly seen that not only the enhancement of luminescence could be achieved through the energy transfer process, but also the performance was significantly improved in LSCs device by increasing the concentration of luminophores. Besides, to exclude reabsorption issues, the edge emission test for LSC devices has been carried out, as shown in Figure S14. Figure S14a shows the schematic diagram of the edge test; Figure S14b collects the PL spectrums at different light spot-detector distances. It can be observed that the corresponding PL intensity appeared to diminish as the distance increased, as expected. Figure S14c shows the normalized PL spectrums. It can be clearly observed that the peak shape does not suffer any appreciable variation, and only a slight red shift in the wavelength of the emission spectrum occurred, meaning that very weak resorption is present. To evaluate the quantum efficiency of light emission from the LSC device, as seen in Figure S15, clear (a1) or masked (a2) edges were used to distinguish between light emitted from the LSC surface and guided light emission from the slab edges. The corresponding quantum efficiencies Q
1 and Q
2 were measured for the two configurations (Figure S15a). Q
1 (clear edges) was 50.6 % and Q
2 (masked edges) was 20.3 % (Figure S15b). Even under one sun illumination (100 mW/cm2), the corresponding η
PCE of an LSC is 1.66 % (Figure S16). Besides, corresponding to different sizes of LSC devices, the corresponding optical conversion efficiencies have almost no effect (Figure S17 and Table S2).

Device stability is also an important parameter for the lab-to-fab transition of LSC technology. The stability of the film LSCs was investigated by measuring the η PCE of LSCs based on CDs@SiO2, RhB@SiO2, and CDs/RhB@SiO2 within 28 days in ambient conditions (Wuhan, China, noon, summer). As shown in Fig. 6, after four weeks, the η PCE of all LSCs had almost no alteration, indicating the good stability of the as-prepared film LSCs, which reveals that silica shell encapsulation can greatly improve the optical stability of the luminescent materials. And the stability of LSC devices under different conditions was shown in Figure S18. Figure S18a shows the PL spectra of the LSC device kept at a constant temperature of 80 °C for 6 h, Figure S18b presents the PL spectra under UV lamp (18 W) with continuous light for 6 h, and Figure S18c exhibits the PL spectra of the LSC device with continuous contact with the air for 6 h in an outdoor area with high humidity (Wuhan, 8:00 a.m. to 14:00 p.m., October 15, 2024, cloudy, no sunlight), and these spectra can be clearly observed that there was almost no change in the PL spectra before and after treatment under these different conditions, indicating that LSC has an excellent stability.

In conclusion, the CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite nanoparticles with energy transfer properties were synthesized by the Stöber method which possesses a high solid-state PLQY of up to 57 %. By optimizing the experimental conditions, a FRET efficiency of ∼ 86 % between CDs and RhB was achieved. Subsequently, the optical characterization showed that the as-prepared CDs/RhB@SiO2 composite nanoparticles had excellent optical properties such as wide UV–vis absorption, high PLQY, large Stokes shift, and good stability. Eventually, originating from this set of favorable optical properties, a series of the film LSCs with various concentrations of CDs/RhB@SiO2 were fabricated by combining them with polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) via a drop coating method. The best η
PCE value of ∼ 3.8 % was successfully achieved and the LSCs possessed excellent stability in ambient conditions. Therefore, by selecting an appropriate donor and acceptor and combining them well, the employment of the energy transfer process is expected to achieve high-performance LSCs and high transfer efficiency luminescent nanocomposite materials since they can maintain or even enhance the original optical properties, which have a promising prospect of application in photoelectric devices. In particular, our reported work is expected to provide new experimental support for the application of energy transfer in LSC devices and open new paths for the next development of LSC devices.

Xiao Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Alberto Vomiero: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jiurong Li: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiujian Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Shuyang Zheng: Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098), and the Ministry of Education, China - 111 Project (No. B18038). A. Vomiero acknowledges the Kempe Foundation, the Wallenberg Foundation, the Vetenskapsrädet under contract 2022–05024 and the Italian MUR under PNRR NEST program for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2024.110514.
